verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 (Hsp60) is a mitochondrial chaperonin canonically resident in the mitochondrial matrix. However, documented evidence establishes surface-accessible pools under specific conditions: (1) cell-death-induced exposure — Hsp60 externalizes to the outer leaflet during apoptosis and is recognized as a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP); (2) cancer-cell ecto-display — tumor cells display Hsp60 on the cell surface where it functions as an ecto-chaperone and immunogenic target; (3) infection-induced translocation — bacterial and viral triggers promote surface display; (4) possible activation-induced secretion in immune contexts. While the baseline canonical compartment is mitochondrial matrix, the protein body does reach the outer face under defined non-baseline cellular states and is an established target of cancer immunotherapy and DAMPs pathways.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Uncertain whether the cell-death and cancer-ecto pools are sufficiently stable and frequent to support therapeutic access under physiologic conditions, or whether they represent transient / rare presentations. The canonical intracellular compartment is unambiguous; the contextual evidence is robust but non-baseline.
